title: Remembering a place that no longer exists
summary: Reflect on a place from your past that has changed or disappeared, and argue whether physical memory or mental memory is more reliable.
topic: A place from your past that has changed or disappeared
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) reflecting on a place you remember clearly but that has since changed or been demolished. Describe what it looked like, explain what made it memorable, and argue whether our mental image of a place is more or less reliable than a photograph. Use the present perfect to describe how the place has changed, and at least one conditional to explore what might have been lost.
hintStarters: The place I keep returning to in my memory is…
Since I last visited, the building has…
If I had taken more photographs at the time, I wonder whether…

hintVocab.meaningVi: bị biến dạng theo thời gian

hintVocab.phrase: to be attached to a place
hintVocab.meaningVi: gắn bó với một nơi chốn
mang điều gì đó theo trong lòng
người kể chuyện không đáng tin cậy
một điểm mốc, công trình tiêu biểu

sampleText:
There was a small cinema in the neighbourhood where I grew up, a narrow building wedged between a hardware shop and a noodle stall, with a handwritten sign above the entrance that changed every week. I have not been back to that street in seven years, but I can still see the cinema clearly — the sticky floor, the single ceiling fan that was always too slow, the way the light from the screen made everyone's face look blue. When I asked my mother about it recently, she told me it had been torn down and replaced by a parking structure. I did not feel sad exactly. I felt disoriented, the way you feel when a word you have used all your life turns out to be spelled differently from how you imagined. It is not grief. It is the very specific confusion of discovering that your map of a place has been quietly corrected without anyone telling you.

What is interesting is that my memory of the cinema has probably been getting less accurate every year since I last walked past it. Each time I recall it, I likely change it slightly — the way a story changes each time it is told aloud, picking up small variations that come to feel like the original. I have added details that may never have been there — a smell of old wood, a particular creak in the third row of seats — and I have almost certainly smoothed over the things that were uncomfortable or dull. Memory does not preserve a place the way a photograph does. It reshapes the place around the emotions you were feeling at the time, so what you remember is not really the cinema but the version of yourself who sat in it.

This raises a question that I have not been able to settle: if I had found a photograph of that cinema taken in the year I used to go there, would it have made my memory stronger or weaker? I think about this often, not only for the cinema but for other places I have lost track of. My instinct is that it would have weakened it. A photograph fixes one angle, one light, one afternoon, and once you have looked at it enough times, it starts to replace the looser, warmer, more personal image that you were carrying. There is something a photograph cannot hold — the specific quality of waiting for a film to start in a room full of strangers who are all, briefly, wanting the same thing. That part of the memory cannot be torn down, even when the building is gone.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect to describe change since a past time
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have not been back to that street in seven years' và 'my memory… has probably been getting less accurate' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để nối một khoảng thời gian trong quá khứ với hiện tại. Cấu trúc 'has/have + past participle' nhấn mạnh rằng hậu quả hoặc trạng thái đó vẫn còn liên quan đến bây giờ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have not been back to that street in seven years' and 'my memory… has probably been getting less accurate' use the present perfect to link a past time to the present. The 'has/have + past participle' structure stresses that the consequence or state is still relevant now.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional to explore an imagined past
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If I had found a photograph… would it have made my memory stronger or weaker?' dùng điều kiện loại 3 (If + past perfect, would + have + past participle) để tưởng tượng điều gì đó đã không xảy ra trong quá khứ và suy ngẫm về kết quả có thể xảy ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If I had found a photograph… would it have made my memory stronger or weaker?' uses the third conditional (If + past perfect, would + have + past participle) to imagine something that did not happen in the past and reflect on what the outcome might have been.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một rạp chiếu phim nhỏ trong khu phố nơi tôi lớn lên, một tòa nhà hẹp bị kẹp giữa một cửa hàng dụng cụ và một quán mì, với một tấm biển viết tay trên lối vào thay đổi mỗi tuần. Tôi đã không quay lại con phố đó trong bảy năm, nhưng tôi vẫn có thể thấy rõ rạp chiếu phim — sàn nhà dính, chiếc quạt trần duy nhất luôn quay quá chậm, cách ánh sáng từ màn hình làm khuôn mặt mọi người trông có màu xanh. Khi tôi hỏi mẹ về nó gần đây, bà nói với tôi rằng nó đã bị phá bỏ và thay thế bởi một bãi đỗ xe. Tôi không cảm thấy buồn hẳn. Tôi cảm thấy mất phương hướng, giống như cảm giác khi một từ bạn đã dùng cả đời hóa ra được đánh vần khác với cách bạn tưởng tượng.

Điều thú vị là ký ức của tôi về rạp chiếu phim có lẽ ngày càng kém chính xác hơn mỗi năm kể từ lần cuối tôi đi ngang qua. Tôi đã thêm vào những chi tiết có thể chưa bao giờ ở đó — mùi gỗ cũ, tiếng kẽo kẹt đặc biệt ở hàng ghế thứ ba — và tôi거의 chắc chắn đã che đi những thứ không thoải mái hay nhàm chán. Ký ức không gìn giữ một nơi chốn như cách một bức ảnh làm. Nó tái tạo lại nơi chốn xung quanh những cảm xúc bạn đang cảm nhận lúc đó, vì vậy những gì bạn nhớ không thực sự là rạp chiếu phim mà là phiên bản của chính bạn đã ngồi trong đó.

Điều này đặt ra một câu hỏi mà tôi vẫn chưa giải quyết được: nếu tôi tìm thấy một bức ảnh chụp rạp chiếu phim đó vào năm tôi thường đến đó, liệu nó có làm ký ức của tôi mạnh hơn hay yếu hơn không? Linh cảm của tôi là nó sẽ làm yếu đi. Một bức ảnh cố định một góc nhìn, một ánh sáng, một buổi chiều, và khi bạn đã nhìn vào nó đủ nhiều lần, nó bắt đầu thay thế hình ảnh thoải mái hơn, ấm áp hơn, cá nhân hơn mà bạn đang mang theo trong lòng. Có điều gì đó một bức ảnh không thể giữ được — cái chất đặc biệt của sự chờ đợi một bộ phim bắt đầu trong một căn phòng đầy người lạ, những người trong chốc lát cùng mong muốn một điều. Phần ký ức đó không thể bị phá bỏ, ngay cả khi tòa nhà đã không còn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a break in contact with a place?
options: I have not been back to that street in seven years.
I haven't went back to that street in seven years.
I have not been back to that street since seven years.
I didn't been back to that street in seven years.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have not been back' is the correct present perfect negative. 'Haven't went' uses simple past participle incorrectly. 'Since seven years' is wrong — 'since' needs a point in time, not a duration. 'Didn't been' mixes simple past and past participle.
mcQuestions.hint: With a duration like 'seven years', the correct preposition is 'in' or 'for', not 'since'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the passive to describe demolition?
options: The cinema had been torn down and replaced by a parking structure.
The cinema had been tore down and replaced by a parking structure.
The cinema has been tear down and replaced by a parking structure.
The cinema was being torn down and replaced by a parking structure.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Had been torn down' = past perfect passive, correct for an event that happened before another past time. 'Tore' is the simple past, not the past participle. 'Tear' is the base form. The last option implies an action in progress at a specific past moment, which doesn't fit.
mcQuestions.hint: The past perfect passive is 'had been + past participle'; the past participle of 'tear' is 'torn'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'probably' correctly as a degree adverb?
options: My memory has probably been getting less accurate every year.
My memory has been probably getting less accurate every year.
My memory probably has been getting less accurate every year.
My memory has been getting probably less accurate every year.
mcQuestions.explanation: Adverbs like 'probably' typically come between the auxiliary verb and the main verb in a perfect progressive construction. Placing it immediately before 'been' or 'less accurate' is awkward in natural English.
mcQuestions.hint: Place 'probably' between the first auxiliary ('has') and the second auxiliary ('been').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'not … exactly' to soften a statement?
options: I did not feel sad exactly — I felt disoriented.
I did not feel sad, exactly — I felt disoriented.
I did not felt sad exactly — I felt disoriented.
I did not feel exactly sad — I felt disoriented.
mcQuestions.explanation: When 'exactly' is used as a qualifier at the end of a clause, a comma before it is correct punctuation and the most natural placement. 'Did not felt' mixes auxiliary and simple past. Moving 'exactly' before 'sad' changes the meaning to 'not precisely sad' which is grammatical but less idiomatic.
mcQuestions.hint: The qualifying 'exactly' sits after the adjective it softens and is often set off by a comma.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the third conditional to imagine an alternative past?
options: If I had found a photograph, it would have made my memory stronger.
If I found a photograph, it would have made my memory stronger.
If I had found a photograph, it will have made my memory stronger.
If I had found a photograph, it would made my memory stronger.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional = 'If + past perfect, … would + have + past participle'. Mixing simple past with 'would have' is the second conditional. 'Will have' is future perfect, wrong here. 'Would made' drops the auxiliary 'have'.
mcQuestions.hint: Third conditional requires past perfect in the 'if' clause and 'would have + past participle' in the result.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase most naturally describes memory becoming inaccurate over time?
options: getting less accurate every year
becoming less and less accurate along the years
getting more inaccurate during the years
lowering in accuracy across years
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Getting less accurate every year' is natural and idiomatic. 'Along the years' is a calque (non-native); the correct phrase is 'over the years'. 'Getting more inaccurate' is grammatical but sounds unnatural for gradual decline. 'Lowering in accuracy' is non-native.
mcQuestions.hint: Choose the phrase with the most natural English preposition and collocation for gradual decline over time.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'since' as a conjunction of time?
options: Memory reshapes the place since you last visited it.
Memory reshapes the place since you last visit it.
Memory reshapes the place since you visited it last.
Memory reshapes the place since you have visited it last.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Since' as a time conjunction is typically followed by simple past ('visited'). 'Last' as a time adverb comes at the end naturally. 'Have visited it last' is awkward because 'since' clauses use simple past, not present perfect, in British English usage here.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'since' as a time conjunction, use simple past and place 'last' at the end of the clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'something' + relative clause correctly?
options: There is something a photograph cannot hold.
There is something which a photograph cannot hold it.
There is something that a photograph cannot holds.
There is something a photograph can not holds.
mcQuestions.explanation: A contact relative clause ('something [that] a photograph cannot hold') is natural and correct with no pronoun repetition. Repeating 'it' inside the clause is a common learner error. 'Cannot holds' adds a third-person 's' incorrectly after a modal.
mcQuestions.hint: In a contact relative clause after 'something', do not repeat the object pronoun inside the clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'the way' to describe a comparison?
options: Memory does not preserve a place the way a photograph does.
Memory does not preserve a place in the way as a photograph does.
Memory does not preserve a place like how a photograph does.
Memory does not preserve a place the same way than a photograph does.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The way' as a conjunction of manner needs no extra 'in' or 'as'. 'Like how' is informal and non-standard in written B1. 'The same way than' is incorrect — comparisons use 'as' not 'than' with 'the same way'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'The way' as a manner conjunction stands alone — no 'in' before it and no 'as' after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'even when' to express a persistent truth despite a condition?
options: That memory cannot be torn down, even when the building is gone.
That memory cannot be torn down, even if the building was gone.
That memory cannot be torn down, even though the building gone.
That memory cannot be torn down, even when the building has gone.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even when the building is gone' — present simple for a general condition. 'Even if the building was gone' uses past tense, suggesting a hypothetical. 'The building gone' omits the verb entirely. 'Has gone' is present perfect, shifting focus to the recent past rather than a general state.
mcQuestions.hint: For a general ongoing condition (not a specific past event), use present simple after 'even when'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'all your life' with the present perfect?
options: a word you have used all your life
a word you used all your life
a word you are using all your life
a word you have been used all your life
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect ('have used') with 'all your life' expresses an action from birth to now. Simple past ('used') cuts the connection to the present. 'Are using' is present progressive, suggesting a temporary current action. 'Have been used' is passive.
mcQuestions.hint: 'All your life' extends to now — that signals present perfect, not simple past.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is it possible that forgetting the exact details of a place makes your memory of it more meaningful, not less? What does accuracy leave out that emotion keeps?
